sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 56-year-old male, presents with difficulty swallowing that began approximately 4-5 weeks ago.
He reports feeling like something is stuck in his throat, which is painful and occasionally causes him to have to wash it down with liquids.
He has never fully choked but has thought about it.
The symptoms are more pronounced with solid foods, and he has not noticed any changes with temperature of the food or liquids.
He denies coughing, vomiting blood, or weight loss.
He also denies any chest pain, shortness of breath, or nausea.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes and high blood pressure.
He is currently on metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes and norvasc for high blood pressure.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms in other organs that are relevant to his initial presentation.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes
Norvasc for high blood pressure
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is 120/50 mmHg.
Physical exam: The patient's neck is fine with no swelling.
The thyroid feels normal, and there are no masses or lymph nodes.
The throat exam is normal with no masses or swelling.
The lung exam is clear, and the heart exam is normal.
There is epigastric tenderness in the right upper quadrant but no masses or significant swelling.
The back exam is normal, and the extremity and neurological exams are fine.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with difficulty swallowing that may be due to a narrowing of the esophagus, possibly causing a stricture.
Problem list: Dysphagia; Hypertension; Diabetes mellitus; Stricture of the esophagus; Gastroesophageal reflux disease (suspected)
Plan:
I will refer the patient to gastroenterology for further evaluation with an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) to investigate the stricture.
I will prescribe prilosec 20mg once a day for gastroesophageal reflux disease.
I will also recommend that the patient chew their foods frequently, especially hard foods like steak or meat, to prevent them from getting stuck.
I will order another hemoglobin A1c and kidney function tests.
I will also refer the patient to ophthalmology to check for diabetic retinopathy.
I will continue the patient on metformin and norvasc as previously prescribed.
I will send a refill for norvasc to the patient's pharmacy.
Follow-up: I will see the patient back in about a month to review the results of the blood tests and make any necessary adjustments to their treatment plan.
